purpose: Convince a mixed public-health team that pathogen genomics is worth considering, while avoiding technology hype.
readerActionAfterReading: Define the public-health decisions that genomic information could improve before choosing tools or infrastructure.
mustCover: Value comes from changed public-health action, not data production alone.
Genomics depends on sampling, epidemiology, metadata, laboratory quality, reporting routes, and response capacity.
Economic claims must be setting-specific and should not imply universal savings.
doNotClaim: Do not say genomics is always cost-saving.
Do not imply sequencing alone detects all outbreaks or replaces epidemiology.
Do not generalize high-income implementation results without context.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Director/funder version should emphasize decision value, scenarios, and economic uncertainty.
Technical readers can see more detail on workflow and data dependencies.
figuresTablesWanted: One simple value-chain figure linking sequence data to interpreted public-health action.
knownGaps: Need structured extraction of economic evaluation comparators and numeric estimates.
reviewNotes: Keep this section publication-like. Avoid noisy inline caveats; put weaker evidence needs in Needs more work.

purpose: Help readers decide what service they are actually building before they choose data systems, workflows, infrastructure, or reports.
readerActionAfterReading: Write down the priority use cases, intended users, result products, turnaround targets, validation boundary, and response route for the service.
mustCover: Routine surveillance, outbreak response, AMR monitoring, repository submission, research support, clinical support, and hybrid services have different operational requirements.
A use case should define who uses the result, what decision changes, how quickly the result is needed, and what downstream action is possible.
The guide should distinguish validated service use from exploratory or research-facing analysis.
doNotClaim: Do not imply that a single service design fits every pathogen or public-health use case.
Do not present research flexibility as equivalent to routine reporting readiness.
Do not recommend a technical platform before the service products and users are defined.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Director/funder readers need service scenarios and decision value.
Bioinformaticians need validation boundary and workflow ownership.
Laboratory leads need sample flow, reporting route, and service readiness.
figuresTablesWanted: Research/project workflow versus routine service comparison.
knownGaps: Need stronger extraction from national strategy documents on use-case prioritisation and service models.
reviewNotes: This should prevent the guide from jumping straight to infrastructure.

purpose: Make sampling strategy visible as a core determinant of whether genomic outputs can answer the public-health question.
readerActionAfterReading: Describe which samples or isolates enter the programme, which are excluded, what bias this creates, and how sampling changes interpretation.
mustCover: Genomic interpretation depends on what was sampled and what was not sampled.
Routine sampling, outbreak-triggered sampling, sentinel surveillance, convenience sampling, and targeted sampling support different claims.
Sampling design affects cluster interpretation, trend analysis, repository value, and claims about representativeness.
doNotClaim: Do not imply that more sequencing automatically means representative surveillance.
Do not give universal sampling fractions or thresholds without source-backed organism-specific evidence.
Do not separate sampling from metadata, reporting, and response capacity.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Enteric and foodborne programmes need outbreak and source-tracking examples.
Respiratory virus programmes need variant monitoring and timeliness examples.
AMR and healthcare-associated infection programmes need sampling-frame and setting-specific interpretation text.
figuresTablesWanted: Sampling strategy versus interpretation limits table.
knownGaps: Need source-backed organism-specific sampling and representativeness material.
reviewNotes: This is likely under-sourced at present; use gap notes where needed.

purpose: Make readers define how the genomic surveillance service will be operated, supported, monitored, and recovered, rather than treating infrastructure as a purchase list.
readerActionAfterReading: Name who owns workflows, compute, storage, identity, validation, monitoring, support, and failure recovery.
mustCover: Cloud/local/HPC/managed platform choices are responsibility models.
A routine service needs routes for failed samples, failed runs, ambiguous analysis, delayed reporting, and service interruption.
Service implementation includes LIMS, validation, accreditation, reporting, staff, and user engagement.
Constraints such as internet reliability, staff availability, IT support, and data residency should change recommendations.
doNotClaim: Do not say cloud is the default best answer.
Do not present laptop workflows as equivalent to validated routine service.
Do not hide support and maintenance responsibilities.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Unreliable internet: raise local/offline/hybrid trade-offs.
No bioinformatics staff: raise managed platform, shared service, or external support text.
Data residency concern or cloud not allowed: lower cloud-first wording and raise governance review.
figuresTablesWanted: Responsibility model table for SaaS/PaaS/IaaS/local/managed service.
knownGaps: Need stronger source-backed procurement and support-model examples from public-health agencies.
reviewNotes: This is central to Gnomey. Keep practical and implementation-oriented.

purpose: Make routine service failure handling explicit, so the guide covers what happens when real samples, runs, workflows, reports, and data submissions go wrong.
readerActionAfterReading: Define local routes for failed samples, failed sequencing runs, low-quality outputs, missing metadata, ambiguous results, corrected reports, and periodic service review.
mustCover: A prospective service must manage exceptions in real time, not only describe the ideal successful path.
Failure handling should connect laboratory process, bioinformatics process, reporting, user communication, and quality review.
Service review should examine turnaround time, failure rates, metadata completeness, pipeline incidents, report corrections, and user feedback.
doNotClaim: Do not prescribe specific failure thresholds without source-backed QC material.
Do not imply that failure handling is only a bioinformatics responsibility.
Do not hide this material only in technical detail.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: No LIMS should raise manual identifier checks and exception logging.
No central IT should raise incident-routing and backup-testing warnings.
Routine-service and national-scale profiles should see more quality-review detail.
figuresTablesWanted: Exception route table for sample, sequencing, analysis, reporting, and sharing failures.
knownGaps: Need stronger extraction from quality-management and laboratory implementation sources.
reviewNotes: This section should turn the guide from a happy-path description into a service guide.

purpose: Separate storage concepts that are often conflated, and make retention and recovery decisions visible.
readerActionAfterReading: Classify each data type as active analysis data, intermediate workflow data, report or record, shared output, archive item, or backup target.
mustCover: Active storage, backup, replication, archive, disaster recovery, retention, and deletion are different decisions.
Raw reads, intermediate files, workflow logs, reports, QC records, reference data, and repository accessions do not need identical rules.
Storage choices affect cost, recovery, auditability, reanalysis, and data sharing.
doNotClaim: Do not define universal retention periods without jurisdictional or institutional source material.
Do not confuse backup with replication or archive.
Do not give storage-size rules without explicitly marking them as context-specific.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Unreliable internet should raise local recovery and offline-first considerations.
Data residency concern should raise controlled storage and governance review.
High-throughput or national-scale profiles should see cost-growth and lifecycle-management detail.
figuresTablesWanted: Storage, backup, archive, and retention distinction table.
knownGaps: Need public-health retention-policy sources before prescribing retention periods.
reviewNotes: This should stay practical and avoid fake precision.

purpose: Give readers concrete implementation models they can compare and adapt rather than leaving them with abstract principles.
readerActionAfterReading: Select one or two plausible implementation patterns and list the dependencies, risks, and responsibilities for each.
mustCover: Central reference laboratory, local laboratory with central bioinformatics, managed platform, collaborative network, low-connectivity local-first, and academic-to-service transition models solve different problems.
Each pattern should state what is operated locally, what is delegated, what could fail, and what evidence supports the example.
Case studies should be examples to adapt, not universal templates.
doNotClaim: Do not rank one implementation model as universally best.
Do not generalise a high-income national implementation without context.
Do not describe a model without naming its dependencies.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: No bioinformatics staff should raise managed or shared-service examples.
Unreliable internet should raise local-first and hybrid examples.
National-scale profiles should raise collaborative network examples.
knownGaps: Need extraction from each case study into comparable dependency fields.
reviewNotes: This is where practical copyable examples should live.

purpose: Keep unresolved evidence needs visible without cluttering the main whitepaper text.
readerActionAfterReading: Understand which recommendations are source-backed, which are partial, and which need more extraction or external review.
mustCover: Evidence gaps should be organised by whitepaper section.
Gaps should name the type of source needed, not just say more evidence is needed.
The section should include organism-specific gaps, figure/table gaps, and source-extraction gaps.
doNotClaim: Do not use the gaps section to introduce unsupported guidance.
Do not hide major limitations only in backstage tooling.
Do not make the public guide look more complete than the evidence base.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Public view should be concise.
Backstage/editorial view can show fuller extraction tasks and source needs.
figuresTablesWanted: Gap inventory grouped by section and severity.
knownGaps: This section should eventually be generated from structured gap records.
reviewNotes: Keep light-touch in the public guide and detailed in backstage.

purpose: Explain the complete operational data service so teams do not optimize only sequencing, one pipeline run, or one final report.
readerActionAfterReading: Map sample receipt, metadata capture, sequence generation, QC, workflow execution, interpretation, reporting products, sharing routes, storage, retention, and feedback loops.
mustCover: A routine service maintains a reliable data asset, not just FASTQ files or a single report.
Sample and metadata linkage must survive handoffs.
The service must handle failed samples, failed runs, low-quality data, unexpected organisms, and ambiguous outputs in real time.
Different users may need different outputs from the same data pool: urgent alerts, weekly reports, monthly summaries, annual trend analysis, repository submissions, and quality dashboards.
Use cases change turnaround time, sharing, QC, and retention needs.
doNotClaim: Do not provide organism-specific QC thresholds until source-backed.
Do not imply one universal lifecycle for every pathogen programme.
conditionalVariantsNeeded: Enteric bacteria and foodborne programmes need repository/submission detail.
Respiratory virus programmes need variant/lineage monitoring and rapid sharing detail.
No LIMS should trigger extra identifier-lineage guidance.
knownGaps: Need formal extraction from repository metadata guidance and organism-specific QC sources.
reviewNotes: Use this as the main place to integrate figures and tables seamlessly.
